section.title: The Locust Plague
section.sectionRationale: The book opens with a catastrophe so severe it must be told to the next generation: wave upon wave of locusts has stripped the land bare. Joel reads this disaster not as random misfortune but as a summons, a herald of the Day of the LORD, and he calls elders, drunkards, priests, and farmers alike to wake, wail, and turn to God.
section.thesis: A devastating locust plague is a trumpet blast: it lays bare the fragility of life apart from God, and it points beyond itself to the nearness of the Day of the LORD, calling the whole community to fast, cry out, and return.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the charge to remember and tell; the fourfold locust and the ruined land; the call to mourn as priests and farmers grieve; and the cry to the LORD as even the beasts pant for Him.

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD that came to Joel son of Pethuel:
Hear this, O elders; and give ear, all who dwell in the land. Has anything like this ever happened in your days or in the days of your fathers?
Tell it to your children; let your children tell it to their children, and their children to the next generation.

What the devouring locust has left, the {{arbeh|swarming locust}} has eaten; what the swarming locust has left, the young locust has eaten; and what the young locust has left, the destroying locust has eaten.
Wake up, you drunkards, and weep; wail, all you drinkers of wine, because of the sweet wine, for it has been cut off from your mouth.
For a nation has invaded My land, powerful and without number; its teeth are the teeth of a lion, and its fangs are the fangs of a lioness.
It has laid waste My grapevine and splintered My fig tree. It has stripped off the bark and thrown it away; the branches have turned white.

units.label: A call to mourning
units.note: the offerings cut off, the land in grief

verses.text: Wail like a virgin dressed in sackcloth, grieving for the husband of her youth.
Grain and drink offerings have been cut off from the house of the LORD; the priests are in mourning, those who minister before the LORD.
The field is ruined; the land mourns. For the grain is destroyed, the new wine is dried up, and the oil fails.
Be dismayed, O farmers, wail, O vinedressers, over the wheat and barley, because the harvest of the field has perished.
The grapevine is dried up, and the fig tree is withered; the pomegranate, palm, and apple, all the trees of the orchard, are withered. Surely the joy of mankind has dried up.

units.label: A call to repentance
units.note: consecrate a fast, cry out to the LORD

verses.text: Put on sackcloth and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar. Come, spend the night in sackcloth, O ministers of my God, because the grain and drink offerings are withheld from the house of your God.
{{qadash|Consecrate}} a fast; proclaim a solemn assembly! Gather the elders and all the residents of the land to the house of the LORD your God, and cry out to the LORD.
Alas for the day! For the {{yomYHWH|Day of the LORD}} is near, and it will come as destruction from the {{shaddai|Almighty}}.

units.label: Even the beasts pant for You
units.note: the cry from a scorched land

verses.text: Has not the food been cut off before our very eyes, joy and gladness from the house of our God?
The seeds lie shriveled beneath the clods; the storehouses are in ruins; the granaries are broken down, for the grain has withered away.
How the cattle groan! The herds wander in confusion because they have no pasture. Even the flocks of sheep are suffering.
To You, O LORD, I call, for fire has consumed the open pastures and flames have scorched all the trees of the field.
Even the beasts of the field pant for You, for the streams of water have dried up, and fire has consumed the open pastures.

arbeh.gloss: The most common Hebrew word for locust, from a root meaning to multiply or be many. It names the vast, teeming swarm that darkens the sky and devours everything green. Joel piles up four terms for the locust in one verse to picture wave after wave of destruction, leaving nothing for the next. The same insect was the eighth plague that fell on Egypt.

qadash.gloss: To set apart as holy, to consecrate or sanctify. To consecrate a fast is to declare a sacred time, marking it off from ordinary life and dedicating it wholly to seeking God. The word calls the whole community to a solemn, God-focused response rather than mere private sorrow.

yomYHWH.gloss: The Day of the LORD, the central theme of Joel. It is the appointed time when God draws near to judge sin and to save His people, a day of darkness for His enemies and deliverance for the repentant. The locust plague is a near preview of that greater and final Day, which is near and comes as destruction from the Almighty.

shaddai.gloss: Almighty, a title of God stressing His overwhelming power. Joel makes a deliberate wordplay: destruction (shod) comes from Shaddai, the Almighty. The One who is more than able to bless is also more than able to bring the ruin that His holiness requires when His people persist in sin.

basic.context: Joel opens with a national emergency: a plague of locusts has swept through the land in waves, eating the crops, the vines, and even the bark from the trees. The grain and wine that fed families and supplied the temple offerings are gone. Joel tells the people this is so unheard of it must be passed down for generations, and he reads it as a warning from God.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened; the fields really were stripped. But Joel does not treat it as bad luck. He sees the disaster as a sign pointing beyond itself, a small and near preview of the great Day of the LORD. His aim is not to explain insect behaviour but to wake a comfortable people and call them to turn back to God.
basic.originalLanguages: Joel stacks four words for locust in verse four to picture relentless wave after wave. He calls the people to consecrate, qadash, a fast, to set apart a sacred time to seek God. And in verse fifteen he names his great theme, the Day of the LORD, yom YHWH, with a pun: destruction, shod, comes from the Almighty, Shaddai.
basic.typology: The locust army that no one can resist foreshadows the greater judgment of the Day of the LORD. The ruined offerings, grain and wine cut off from God's house, point ahead to the true bread and true vine, Jesus, who is never cut off from His people. The scorched land groaning for water anticipates the world's longing for the restoration only God can bring.
basic.christ: The plague strips away every false security and leaves people crying out to God, which is exactly where the gospel meets us. Jesus is the true grain and true vine that the locusts could never touch, the offering that is never cut off. He bore the destruction from the Almighty in our place so that the Day of the LORD becomes, for those who trust Him, a day of rescue.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over locusts; He can use the smallest creatures to humble a nation. He is holy and will not let sin go unaddressed, so He sends the plague as a wake-up call. Yet the very fact that He warns, and invites the people to cry out to Him, shows a God who disciplines in order to draw His people back, not to destroy them.
basic.crossReferences: The locusts recall the eighth plague on Egypt, Exodus 10. The Day of the LORD is developed by Amos, Zephaniah, and others among the Twelve. Jesus calls Himself the true vine, John 15, and the bread of life, John 6, the harvest the locust can never spoil.
basic.oneStory: Joel's plague is one scene in the long story of a God who lets ruin fall so that people will stop trusting in bread and wine and turn to Him. It points forward to the true harvest, Jesus, and to the final Day when God will judge all evil and satisfy His people forever.

context.literary: Joel opens without the usual dating by kings, plunging straight into a vivid poem of disaster. The chapter builds by waves: the fourfold locust, the ruined vines and fields, the mourning of priests and farmers, and finally a direct cry to God. The imagery is agricultural and liturgical at once, moving from the field to the house of the LORD, so that a natural catastrophe becomes a spiritual summons.
context.historical: Joel gives few historical markers, which is part of why its date is debated, but the setting is clearly agrarian Judah with a functioning temple, priesthood, and system of daily grain and drink offerings. A locust plague in such a society meant not only hunger but the collapse of worship, since the offerings depended on the harvest. The disaster touched every level of life, from the drunkard's wine to the priest's altar.
context.canonical: Joel stands among the Twelve, the Minor Prophets, and is often placed to develop the theme of the Day of the LORD that runs through Amos, Obadiah, Zephaniah, and Malachi. Chapter one sets up the whole book: the locusts of chapter one become the pattern for the army of chapter two, and the call to return here prepares for the promise of restoration and the outpouring of the Spirit that follow.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To interpret a real and devastating locust plague as a divine summons, showing that the disaster is a near and lesser preview of the Day of the LORD, and so to move the whole community, from drunkards to priests, to fast, lament, and cry out to God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical calamity and Judah's need to respond; it does not teach that every natural disaster is a direct punishment for a particular sin. What abides is the pattern: God is sovereign over creation, disasters can be summons to humility, and the right response to loss is to turn to God rather than to despair or numb the pain.

debates.question: Are the locusts a literal plague, a picture of an invading army, or an apocalyptic image?

views.label: A literal locust plague
views.case: The detailed agricultural damage, vines, figs, grain, and the naming of four kinds of locusts, reads most naturally as a real swarm that Joel then uses as a sign of something greater.

views.label: A symbol of a human army
views.case: The locusts are called a nation with teeth like a lion, and chapter two describes them scaling walls like soldiers, so some read them as a coded picture of an invading army such as Assyria or Babylon.

views.label: An apocalyptic foreshadowing
views.case: Because the plague is tied so closely to the Day of the LORD, others see it as pointing beyond any single event to the final judgment, the locusts prefiguring end-time armies as in Revelation 9.

debates.bearing: The readings are not mutually exclusive: a literal plague can genuinely function as a sign of an army and of the final Day. However one weighs the imagery, Joel's point holds, this loss is a call to read God's hand and return to Him.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's force lies in its words. Verse four heaps up four terms for the locust, one of them arbeh, from a root meaning to multiply, to picture successive, total devastation. The response Joel commands is to qadash a fast, to consecrate it, setting apart a holy assembly to seek God with the whole community present. The theological centre arrives in verse fifteen: the yom YHWH, the Day of the LORD, is near, and it comes keshod mishaddai, as destruction from the Almighty, a pointed wordplay in which shod, ruin, sounds out of Shaddai, the Almighty, so that the name of power and the word for devastation echo each other.

typology.fulfillment: The greater and final judgment of the Day of the LORD, before which no human power can stand.

typology.type: Grain and drink offerings cut off from the house of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true grain and true vine, the offering that is never cut off, who restores true worship.

typology.type: The scorched land and beasts panting for water
typology.fulfillment: A groaning creation longing for the renewal God brings in Christ, Romans 8:19-22.

study.christ: The plague does its work by stripping away every comfort, wine, food, harvest, joy, until the only word left is a cry to the LORD. That is the posture in which the gospel meets sinners. Jesus is the true vine that no locust can strip, John 15:1, and the bread of life that never fails, John 6:35, the harvest and offering that endure. The destruction from the Almighty that Joel warns of fell fully on Christ at the cross, so that for all who cry out to the LORD the coming Day is not ruin but rescue. Where Joel's land groans for water, Jesus gives living water that never runs dry, John 7:37-38.
study.god: Joel shows a God who is sovereign over the smallest creatures, able to humble a whole nation with insects, and who is holy, unwilling to let a complacent people drift on unwarned. The plague is not petty cruelty but a summons; the God who sends it is the same God who invites the people to consecrate a fast and cry out to Him. His discipline is aimed at restoration, and even in judgment He leaves the door of repentance wide open.

crossReferences.note: The eighth plague on Egypt was a locust swarm, a precedent for reading locusts as an instrument of God's judgment.
The Day of the LORD is darkness and not light, developing the warning Joel sounds here.
Jesus is the true vine, the source of fruitfulness that the locust of judgment cannot destroy.
Creation groans and longs for renewal, echoing the land and beasts that mourn in Joel 1.

study.oneStory: Joel's ruined fields belong to the long biblical story of a God who lets false securities fail so that His people will turn from bread and wine to Him. The offerings cut off here anticipate the one offering never cut off, Christ, the true grain and vine. The near Day of the LORD points to the final Day, which for those who cry out to the LORD becomes not destruction but the harvest of salvation.

sermon.why: No expository sermon on Joel 1:1-20 from a trusted expositor could be verified by web search this session; a passage-level exposition can be added later once confirmed.

quiz.q: How does Joel want the people to understand the locust plague?

options.text: As a divine summons and a near preview of the Day of the LORD, calling them to return to God.
options.note: Joel reads the disaster as a sign pointing beyond itself to God's judgment and call.

options.text: As a serious agricultural loss they must simply endure and rebuild from.
options.note: The loss is real, but Joel's point is spiritual, not merely economic.

options.text: As bad luck with no meaning behind it.
options.note: Joel insists the plague carries a message from God, not mere chance.

options.text: As proof that God has abandoned His people forever.
options.note: The plague is a call to return, and the book moves on to promised restoration.

quiz.q: Why does Joel stack four different words for locust in verse 4?

options.text: To picture wave after wave of total devastation, leaving nothing behind.
options.note: The piled-up terms convey relentless, complete destruction.

options.text: To identify four separate species with scientific precision.
options.note: The exact species are uncertain; the point is the totality of the ruin, not taxonomy.

options.text: To show the plague came in stages over several seasons of loss.
options.note: Successive waves are implied, but the main thrust is the completeness of the destruction.

options.text: To suggest the damage was actually quite minor.
options.note: The repetition stresses overwhelming, not minor, loss.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the grain and drink offerings being cut off (verse 9)?

options.text: Worship itself has collapsed, since the offerings depended on the ruined harvest.
options.note: The disaster reaches into the house of the LORD, halting the daily offerings.

options.text: The priests have simply lost part of their personal income.
options.note: There is real loss to the priests, but the deeper issue is the breakdown of worship.

options.text: The people have chosen to stop worshiping God.
options.note: The offerings stop because the harvest failed, not by a deliberate refusal to worship.

options.text: It shows the offerings never mattered to God anyway.
options.note: The offerings mattered; their loss is precisely why the priests mourn.

quiz.q: What response does Joel call the whole community to make?

options.text: To consecrate a fast, gather in solemn assembly, and cry out to the LORD.
options.note: Joel summons everyone to set apart a sacred time and turn to God together.

options.text: To grieve privately until the crops eventually recover.
options.note: Sorrow is right, but Joel calls for a public, God-directed turning, not just private grief.

options.text: To organize better defenses against the next plague.
options.note: The call is spiritual repentance, not disaster planning.

options.text: To ignore the loss and carry on drinking as before.
options.note: Joel wakes the drunkards to weep; complacency is exactly what he confronts.

quiz.q: What is the wordplay in verse 15, 'destruction from the Almighty'?

options.text: Shod, destruction, sounds out of Shaddai, the Almighty, tying the ruin to God's own power.
options.note: The Hebrew pun links devastation directly to the character and hand of God.

options.text: It simply names two unrelated ideas that happen to appear together.
options.note: The pairing is a deliberate sound-play, not a coincidence.

options.text: It hints that the destruction is powerful, without any link to God's name.
options.note: The power is real, but the point is that the ruin flows from Shaddai Himself.

options.text: It means God is unable to prevent the coming ruin.
options.note: The Almighty is the source of the judgment, not powerless before it.

questions: Joel calls a comfortable people to wake up and see God's hand in their loss. Where might God be using a disruption in your life to get your attention?
The plague stripped away wine, food, and joy until only a cry to God remained. What securities are you leaning on that could be stripped away, and what would it look like to lean on God instead?
Joel summoned the whole community, from drunkards to priests, to fast and pray together. How does corporate repentance differ from private regret, and where might your church need it?
The offerings were cut off, yet in Christ the true offering is never cut off. How does knowing Jesus as the bread and vine that never fail steady you when earthly provision feels fragile?
Even the beasts pant for God in the drought. When life dries up, do you instinctively cry out to the LORD, or do you turn to something else first? What would change that?

translationNotes.note: The four Hebrew terms for locust are difficult to distinguish precisely; BSB renders them devouring, swarming, young, and destroying. The uncertainty of the exact species does not affect the meaning: successive waves of total devastation.
Where BSB prints an em dash around 'all the trees of the orchard,' it has been rendered with commas per house style, with no change to the meaning.
The phrase 'destruction from the Almighty' renders a Hebrew wordplay, shod (destruction) echoing Shaddai (Almighty); Shaddai is a title of God stressing His overwhelming power.
Where BSB prints an em dash after 'before our very eyes,' it has been rendered as a comma per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Joel 1:1-20. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; where BSB prints em dashes they have been rendered as commas or semicolons per house style with no change to meaning.
